steps.title: Step 1
steps.content: Exponential growth follows y = a \cdot b^t, where a is the initial amount and b is the growth factor per time period.

steps.title: Step 2
steps.content: Bacteria double every hour, so b = 2. The initial count is 66,000, so a = 66,000.

steps.title: Step 4
steps.content: Check: at t = 0, y = 66,000 \cdot 1 = 66,000 \checkmark; at t = 1, y = 132,000 (doubled). \checkmark

explanation:
• Choice A — ‘$y=\frac{1}{2}(66,000)^{t}$’: This raises 66,000 to the power t and multiplies by 1/2 — not an exponential growth model. At t = 2 this gives an astronomically large number unrelated to doubling. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘$y=2(66,000)^{t}$’: Raises the initial population to the power t, making t = 1 give 2 × 66,000 = 132,000 coincidentally, but t = 2 gives 2 × 66,000² ≈ 8.7 × 10&sup9;, not 264,000. Wrong structure. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘$y=66,000(\frac{1}{2})^{t}$’: The initial value is correct but the growth factor 1/2 means the population halves each hour (exponential decay), not doubles. ✗
  • Choice D — ‘$y=66,000(2)^{t}$’: Initial value 66,000 × growth factor 2 raised to t hours. At t = 1: 132,000; at t = 2: 264,000. Doubles each hour. ✓
That confirms D is correct.
